one_line_claim: DECO is a ReLU-routed sparse MoE with learnable expert-wise scaling, NormSiLU, and non-gated experts that claims dense-comparable quality at the same total parameters and training tokens while activating about 20% of routed experts and delivering up to 3.00x decoding speedup on Jetson AGX.
what_they_tested: They train dense LLaMA-style baselines, DECO, TopP, DeepSeek-V3-style, ReMoE, and BlockFFN-style MoE variants at 0.11B, 0.24B, 0.53B, and 1.18B total parameters. Within each scale they hold training tokens, non-FFN components, routed-expert activation ratio, shared-expert dimension, and expert granularity roughly fixed. Evaluation uses C4 validation perplexity and average accuracy over PIQA, SIQA, HellaSwag, ARC-E, ARC-C, WinoGrande, and LAMBADA. They also run ablations on expert-wise router scaling, NormSiLU, expert gating, activation ratio, shared expert size, expert granularity, a FineWeb data setting, and a custom inference kernel on RTX 4090 and Jetson AGX.
strongest_point: The central comparison is unusually relevant for end-side MoE: same total parameter budget and same token budget against dense models, not the easier compute-budget comparison where sparse MoEs can see more data. The paper also makes the architectural story testable with component ablations and reports both quality and real hardware throughput.
weakest_point: The headline dense-comparable and end-side claims are narrower than the title suggests. The largest model is only 1.18B parameters, margins over dense are often tiny and reported without seed variance, post-training is not tested, and the 3.00x speedup is against a specific baseline/kernel setup rather than a broad optimized deployment comparison.
missing_baselines: A matched-active-compute dense model or smaller dense model trained under the same active FLOP/inference budget, to separate parameter efficiency from compute efficiency.
Strong edge-deployment baselines such as dense models with quantization, pruning, activation sparsity, distillation, or structured FFN sparsification under the same memory and latency budgets.
Optimized dense inference kernels and standard optimized MoE kernels across the same batch sizes, sequence lengths, devices, and precision settings, not just the paper's Baseline AR setup.
A direct DeepSeek-V3-style or DeepSeekMoE implementation tuned as aggressively as DECO under exactly the same total-parameter, token, and active-ratio constraints.
Larger open LLM scales and instruction-tuned models, because the paper's largest pretraining run is 1.18B and the limitations explicitly exclude SFT/RL.
missing_ablations: Run multiple random seeds or repeated trainings for the main dense-vs-DECO comparisons; the reported deltas are small enough that variance matters.
Report active parameters, FLOPs, memory traffic, and wall-clock training/inference cost for each model, including shared experts and attention, rather than only routed-expert activation ratio.
Separate the cost and benefit of NormSiLU's extra average projection and RMS normalization at inference time, including kernel overhead and memory access.
Evaluate activation-ratio, shared-expert-size, and granularity sweeps on downstream tasks and latency, not mainly C4 perplexity plots.
Ablate the adaptive sparsity regularization update rule, target ratio, and entropy loss against simpler L1/L0-style sparsity controls.
Test data-mixture sensitivity beyond one FineWeb small/medium comparison, especially since the discussion says heterogeneous data may make dense comparability easier.
Run SFT/RL or instruction-following evaluations to test whether flexible ReLU routing stays stable after post-training.
red_flags: The paper repeatedly frames DECO as end-side, but storage overhead is mostly argued from matched total parameter count; there is little direct measurement of disk/flash movement, mobile/NPU deployment, memory residency, battery, or model loading behavior.

The dense-comparable margins are often very small: for example Figure 3 reports Small PPL 34.36 vs Dense 34.40 and Large task average 42.81 vs Dense 42.80, with no confidence intervals or seed variance.
Some individual benchmarks move against the headline average, such as DECO Large LAMBADA 25.50 vs Dense 29.34 and DECO XLarge ARC-E 62.96 vs Dense 63.38 in the appendix tables.

The authors modify BlockFFN by replacing its original regularization with their adaptive sparsity regularization for fairness, which makes the baseline less directly comparable to the published method.

The hyperparameter story favors DECO unless baseline tuning budgets are documented in equal detail; Appendix A says data mixing ratios were tuned on small models and hyperparameters were grid-searched then extrapolated.
The abstract says codes and checkpoints will be released, so the current claims are not yet independently reproducible from the paper alone.

The theoretical support for NormSiLU starts from a simplified global layer-normalization argument, then asserts that the implemented dual-stage sparse version preserves the stability property; that bridge is plausible but not fully proven.
The phrase 'activating only 20% of experts' can overstate total compute savings because shared experts, attention, embeddings, and other non-FFN components remain active.
positive_signals: The main experimental protocol explicitly holds total parameter count and training token count fixed within each scale, which directly targets the paper's central claim.
Baselines cover several relevant routing families: TopP, DeepSeek-V3-style, ReMoE, and BlockFFN, plus a dense LLaMA-style reference.
Ablations are concrete and mostly aligned with the proposed components: vector router scaling, NormSiLU's mean/RMS pieces, and gated vs non-gated experts.
The paper acknowledges conditional dense comparability and studies activation ratio, shared expert size, and expert granularity rather than pretending the effect is universal.
The FineWeb experiment weakens the authors' own story in a useful way: DECO Small has slightly worse PPL than Dense on the less heterogeneous dataset while task accuracy remains close.
The hardware section includes actual throughput on RTX 4090 and Jetson AGX instead of only theoretical sparsity or FLOP estimates.
Appendix D gives per-benchmark scores, making it possible to see where average task gains hide regressions.
verdict: A solid Paper Radio pick with a clear technical hook: can sparse MoE be useful when total storage, not just active compute, is constrained? The evidence is promising because the comparisons are token- and parameter-matched and the component ablations are meaningful. The critique should be firm: this is a small-scale pretraining result with tiny quality margins, no variance, no post-training, promised-but-not-yet-released artifacts, and an end-side deployment claim that needs broader memory/latency baselines. Treat it as a credible architectural candidate, not a settled result for production edge LLMs.
citations: Abstract: DECO is claimed to match dense performance under identical total parameter budgets and training tokens, activate only 20% of experts, and achieve a 3.00x hardware speedup; code and checkpoints are promised for release.
Sec. 3.1-3.3: the method combines ReLU-based routing, learnable expert-wise router scaling, non-gated MLP experts, NormSiLU, and adaptive entropy-based sparsity regularization.
Sec. 4.1: experiments compare Dense, TopP, DeepSeek-V3, ReMoE, BlockFFN, and DECO at Small 0.11B, Medium 0.24B, Large 0.53B, and XLarge 1.18B total parameters.

Sec. 4.1: within each scale, training tokens are held at about 40x parameter count; non-FFN components are identical; MoE settings share around 20% routed-expert activation ratio, shared-expert dimension, and close expert parameter counts.
Figure 3: DECO reports better or comparable C4 PPL and average downstream task scores than Dense at all four scales, such as XLarge PPL 18.38 vs Dense 18.41 and XLarge task 47.38 vs Dense 46.98.

Tables 1-3: ablations show DECO beating fixed/scalar router scaling, NormSiLU outperforming SiLU and partial normalizations, and non-gated DECO strongly outperforming gated DECO at Small and Medium scales.

Sec. 5: the authors show dense comparability depends on activation ratio, shared expert size, and expert granularity; Medium reaches dense-level PPL at about 10% activation while Small needs about 15%.
Table 4 and Sec. 6: the custom kernel reaches average decoding speedups of 2.58x on RTX 4090 and 3.00x on Jetson AGX relative to Baseline AR in FR-Spec.
Table 5 and Sec. 7: on FineWeb, DECO Small has slightly worse PPL than Dense, 3.406 vs 3.393, and the authors suggest heterogeneous data may make dense comparability easier.
Limitations: the paper does not test SFT or RL and explicitly leaves open how activation-ratio thresholds, intrinsic sparsity, data distributions, and inference tasks affect robustness.
Appendix D: per-benchmark results reveal hidden regressions, including DECO Large LAMBADA 25.50 vs Dense 29.34 despite almost equal average task accuracy.
